sections.heading: Why irregular verbs exist
sections.body: Most English irregular past tense forms are descendants of Old English and Germanic strong verbs, which changed their internal vowel to mark tense (called ablaut). Regular '-ed' endings came later and became the default for new verbs. Irregular verbs survive because they're the most commonly used.

sections.heading: The main patterns
sections.body: **Vowel change (ablaut):** sing/sang/sung, drink/drank/drunk, swim/swam/swum. **-ought group:** buy/bought, think/thought, bring/brought, catch/caught, teach/taught. **Same form all three:** put/put/put, cut/cut/cut, hit/hit/hit, let/let/let. **Totally unpredictable:** go/went (from a different Old English word), be/was/were/been.

sections.heading: The top 20 you must know
sections.body: be, have, do, go, say, get, make, know, think, take, come, see, want, look, use, find, give, tell, become, show — all irregular. These 20 verbs account for a huge proportion of all English usage.

exercises.instruction: Write the simple past of each verb.

quiz.question: Which verb follows the same vowel-change pattern as 'sing/sang/sung'?
quiz.explanation: 'Ring/rang/rung' follows the exact same ablaut pattern as 'sing/sang/sung'.
